question: What status code should DELETE /api/users/:id return on successful deletion?

options.text: 204 No Content -- successful deletion, no response body needed
200 OK with body { deleted: true }
options.explanation: 200 OK is acceptable but 204 No Content is standard practice for DELETE. The client doesn't need a response body: it knows the resource is deleted from the status code

options.text: 202 Accepted -- deletion accepted for processing
options.explanation: 202 is for asynchronous operations (queued). For synchronous deletion, the correct response is 204 No Content

options.text: 410 Gone -- the resource no longer exists
options.explanation: 410 Gone means a resource existed but is permanently removed. It's not a successful DELETE response code, but an informational status for future GET requests

question: Why is cursor-based pagination more stable than offset-based with concurrent inserts?

options.text: Cursor references a specific record (WHERE id > N), while offset counts from the beginning -- inserts shift all subsequent pages
Cursor-based pagination locks the table from inserts
options.explanation: Cursor-based doesn't lock the table. Stability comes from binding to a specific value (id > 40), not an ordinal number (OFFSET 40)

options.text: Cursor caches the result on the server
options.explanation: Cursor doesn't cache results -- it stores a position (usually an ID). Stability lies in WHERE id > 40 returning the same next records regardless of inserts at the beginning

options.text: Offset-based is slower and therefore skips records
options.explanation: Speed isn't the cause of skips. The problem is that OFFSET 40 shifts when a new record is inserted: record #40 becomes #41, and the user sees it twice or misses it

question: How should multi-field sorting be implemented in a REST API?

options.text: ?sort=-createdAt,name -- comma-separated fields, minus means DESC
?sortBy=createdAt&sortOrder=DESC&sortBy2=name&sortOrder2=ASC
options.explanation: Numbered parameters (sortBy2, sortOrder2) are unwieldy and don't scale. The standard convention: sort=-field1,field2 -- compact and extensible

options.text: ?sort={"createdAt":"desc","name":"asc"}
options.explanation: JSON in query params is hard to read, encode, and cache. The sort=-field1,field2 convention is simpler and widely supported

options.text: ?orderBy=createdAt DESC, name ASC (SQL syntax)
options.explanation: SQL syntax in query params is dangerous (SQL injection), inconvenient to parse, and non-standard. Use sort=-createdAt,name

question: What is the most popular API versioning approach and why?

options.text: URL versioning (/api/v1/) -- simple, clear, easy to cache and proxy
Header versioning (X-API-Version) -- doesn't pollute the URL
options.explanation: Header versioning is valid but less popular: version isn't visible in the URL, harder to test in browsers and document. URL versioning is simpler for most teams

options.text: Content negotiation (Accept header) -- follows HTTP standards
options.explanation: Content negotiation is most spec-correct but complex to use: difficult Accept header, harder to debug. URL versioning wins on simplicity

options.text: No versioning needed -- backward compatibility is sufficient
options.explanation: Backward compatibility is ideal but unrealistic for major changes (field renames, structure changes). Versioning is a necessary tool

question: What headers should be sent when deprecating an API version?

options.text: Deprecation: true -- signal that the version is deprecated
Sunset: <date> -- date after which the version will stop working
Link: </api/v2/users>; rel="successor-version" -- link to the new version
X-Deprecated-Since: <date> -- non-standard header with date
options.explanation: X-Deprecated-Since is a non-standard header. Use standard ones: Deprecation (RFC 8594) and Sunset (RFC 8594) to notify clients
